name: Innovation and Entrepreneurship Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), and the captured course pool and metrics are identical to those of the Economics Minor (both carry id suffix 712), so the entrepreneurship course list is not individually curated. A genuine entrepreneurship spine is present within the shared pool (ENTR 210 -> ENTR 320 Financing New Venture / ENTR 330 Entrepreneurial Strategy -> ENTR 440 Lean Startup Accelerator, plus MGMT 480 Managing Technology and Innovation and MRKT 338 New Product Design), but it is embedded in a much larger unrelated set with no defined structure.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an I&E-specific structure centered on ENTR 210/320/330/440, MGMT 480, and MRKT 338, and de-duplicate it from the Economics Minor so the two distinct minors no longer share the same course pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The entrepreneurship prerequisite chain is sensible and appropriately shallow (ENTR 320 and ENTR 330 require ENTR 410 or ENTR 210; ENTR 440 has none), which suits a minor, and grade conditions elsewhere are clear (deepestPrereqChain 6 comes from finance courses in the shared pool). Internal consistency is weakened by eight dangling references, six of them graduate courses (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) absent from the undergraduate catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the six graduate-level references and confirm the entrepreneurship electives form the intended ladder (ENTR 210 as gateway into financing, strategy, and the lean-startup accelerator).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. A minor should certify a defined competency, and there is no statement of the innovation/entrepreneurship competency this minor certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes (e.g., identify and validate a venture opportunity, build a business model and financing plan, and apply lean-startup customer-discovery methods).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than an imbalance, but a prospective student cannot see the credit requirement or how required and elective credits are split.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total minor credits and list which ENTR and related courses are required versus elective, with credits for each.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is incomplete: no plan of study, no credits, no description, and a pool that is identical to the Economics Minor's (both id-712) and includes a thin placeholder (BME 498, title 'ST:' with an empty description) and graduate-course references. A reader cannot tell what distinguishes this minor from the co-listed one.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a distinct I&E minor page with a description and the curated ENTR-centered course list and credits, and fix the shared-pool duplication with the Economics Minor.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuine entrepreneurship courses are current and practice-oriented: ENTR 440 Lean Startup Accelerator uses lean-startup customer-discovery methodology, ENTR 320 Financing New Venture covers venture funding, ENTR 330 covers entrepreneurial strategy, MGMT 480 covers technology and innovation management, and MRKT 338 covers new product development. This is a strong, contemporary vocational core for an I&E minor. It is not exemplary because, with no defined structure and a pool shared with another minor, there is no guarantee a student assembles the intended sequence.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended I&E track (ENTR 210 -> ENTR 320/330 -> ENTR 440 capstone) so students deliberately move from opportunity recognition through financing to a launch-focused accelerator.

descriptionQuality.justification: The entrepreneurship and innovation descriptions are specific and practice-oriented (ENTR 440 on the lean-startup methodology and acquiring real customers, ENTR 320 on estimating and securing venture funding, MGMT 480 on strategic technology management). The score is held down by the BME 498 placeholder ('ST:' with an empty description) present in the shared pool.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the entrepreneurship courses are clear and sensible (ENTR 320/330 require ENTR 410 or ENTR 210). Internal consistency is reduced by eight dangling references, six of them graduate courses absent from the undergraduate catalog, carried over from the shared pool.

missingTopics.topic: Entrepreneurial finance depth (VC, valuation, cap tables)
missingTopics.rationale: ENTR 320 introduces venture financing, but there is no deeper course on venture-capital structures, startup valuation, or cap-table mechanics that founders increasingly need.
NJIT offers ENGR 350 Intellectual Property for Engineers, but no IP/commercialization course appears in this minor's pool, a notable gap for a technology-oriented I&E credential.
MRKT 338 New Product Design is present, but a dedicated design-thinking/customer-discovery methods course would strengthen the front end of the innovation process.
There is no course on social ventures or impact/mission-driven entrepreneurship, a rapidly growing area of entrepreneurship education.
The pool covers launch (ENTR 440 lean startup) but not the operations of scaling a growing venture or structured go-to-market strategy.

emergingTrends.rationale: Building on MGMT 480 and the lean-startup approach (ENTR 440), an AI/no-code venture-building elective would reflect how new firms are increasingly launched with AI tooling.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital platforms and marketplace business models
emergingTrends.rationale: Modern venture models built on platforms and marketplaces are not explicitly covered and are central to today's high-growth startups.
Green ventures and ESG-driven startups are a high-growth space that the minor could position for with a dedicated elective.
MGMT 480 anchors technology-and-innovation management; extending it to intrapreneurship would serve students who will innovate inside established firms.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-driven customer discovery and growth analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: Combining ENTR 440's lean-startup customer discovery with analytics (MGMT 216) would give students evidence-based venture-growth skills aligned with current practice.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's required/elective structure and total credits (requirementGroups empty; all credit fields null).

De-duplicate the shared course pool: this minor and the Economics Minor resolve to an identical course list and metrics (both id-712), so curate an I&E-specific list centered on ENTR 210/320/330/440, MGMT 480, and MRKT 338.
Remove the six graduate-level dangling references (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) and the BME 498 empty placeholder from the pool.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes for the entrepreneurship competency the minor certifies.
Add IP/technology-commercialization and a growth/scaling course, and modernize toward AI-enabled and sustainable entrepreneurship.
